title: The bacterial growth curve
author: OpenDiscover
blurb: Drop a few microbes into a flask of broth and this is the shape their numbers trace — the single most recognisable curve in microbiology. It starts almost flat while the cells settle in, then rounds into a steep, near-exponential climb as they double and double again, and finally bends over and flattens into a plateau as food and room run out. That ceiling is the carrying capacity, the most the flask can hold; the steepest point in the middle sits at exactly half of it. The whole S is captured by one tug-of-war equation — grow fast when there is room, slow to a stop when there is not — first written down by Pierre-François Verhulst in 1838. The same curve times a batch of yeast in a brewery, warns when food left out will spoil, predicts when an infection peaks, and sets when to harvest a vat of engineered cells making insulin or a vaccine. Read off the doubling time near the start, the moment of fastest growth, and when the culture reaches 90% of capacity — the usual cue to harvest.
